_meta.description: Annotations for DHS AI Inventory Explorer. Two types: classNotes (from course taxonomy) and watchdog (from external analyses). Edit this file to add/remove/update flags without touching the app.

DHS-2408.classNote: Predicts which noncitizens in the Alternatives to Detention program will abscond. Scores 1–5. Illustrates multiple bias types simultaneously: Historical bias (who was in ATD-ISAP historically reflects enforcement priorities), Measurement bias (‘absconded’ flattens intentional flight, missed hearings, and system failures into one label), Deployment bias (framed as ‘additional insight’ but law precludes any appeal), and Automation bias (officers reviewing thousands of cases rely on the score). Uses Sex/Gender and Age.

watchdog.concern: Appropriately designated rights-impacting, but inventory notes risks of ‘incorrectly giving high scores’ and ‘unrepresentative training data’ with inadequate mitigation details provided.
Used for detention decisions without notifying impacted individuals or their attorneys of AI involvement. Lacks transparency in decision-making.

Specifically named in letter calling on DHS to suspend non-compliant AI tools. Estimates detainee’s flight risk — coalition argues it violates federal responsible AI requirements for life-impacting decisions.

DHS-2556.classNote: Built ‘to solve the problem of human bias during resume reviews.’ Uses off-the-shelf GPT-4 to score candidates red/yellow/green/blue. No custom training. A general-purpose LLM deployed as a consequential HR tool. Appeal process: in-progress.

DHS-130.classNote: Scans narrative sections of immigration applications for ‘patterns that may indicate potential fraud, national security, and/or public safety concerns.’ Claims to be ‘agnostic’ about whether patterns indicate fraud — but what makes a pattern suspicious in narrative text? Could flag writing styles or cultural expressions associated with certain national origins.

DHS-2515.classNote: An LLM summarizes and categorizes incoming tips, translating non-English tips to English regardless of original language. Framed as efficiency, but categorization IS prioritization — the system determines which tips officers see first.

DHS-407.classNote: Facial recognition for ATD-ISAP check-ins via smartphone app (SmartLINK). Pass or ‘pending review.’ Training data includes ‘diverse facial images’ but the vendor controls it. If the system fails more often for certain skin tones or lighting conditions, those people get flagged more — and the output says ‘pending review,’ not ‘I’m not sure.’

DHS-2578.classNote: Uses LLMs to extract addresses from rap sheets and warrants for Enforcement and Removal Operations officers. If the LLM misreads an address, that’s a wrong-door situation. Consequential output with no obvious quality check described in the inventory.

DHS-2457.classNote: Compares photos to ‘publicly available online images’ to locate missing people, including unaccompanied minors who crossed the border. Appeal: ‘Not applicable.’ The same facial recognition technology used for enforcement is also used for protection — same tool, different framing.

watchdog.concern: Social media monitoring tool that ‘searches and aggregates social media data’ to screen travelers. Raises surveillance and discrimination concerns without adequate transparency.
DHS sought extensions for this social media monitoring program despite civil rights concerns.

ARGOS fraud risk analysis. Inventory itself lists known risks: ‘data collection bias, lack of domain-specific accuracy, limited generalization to unseen data.’ But risk mitigation explanations are ‘indecipherable to an outsider.’

Mobile Fortify (CBP version). Listed as high-impact and actively deployed in immigration enforcement, but ICE still working on completing an AI impact assessment. Potential impacts ‘have yet to be identified’ — despite active deployment.
Mobile Fortify (ICE version). Same tool as CBP’s DHS-2731, deployed by ICE. AI impact assessment incomplete despite active use in immigration enforcement.

ICE real-time translation tool. Despite DHS determination that it doesn’t impact rights, the tool likely communicates ‘rights to migrants, agency expectations, and critical deadlines that, if missed, may waive a person’s rights.’

DHS-2389.classNote: Passenger Security Assessment Model. Uses Sex/Gender and Age to produce risk assessments integrated into the Automated Targeting System. What does ‘security risk’ actually measure?

watchdog.concern: Integrates AI outputs into the Automated Targeting System (ATS), ‘creating compounded civil rights risks through integration with problematic automated systems.’

DHS-365.classNote: The only system in the entire inventory that explicitly uses Race/Ethnicity, Sex/Gender, AND Age as model inputs. Facial recognition for visa processing via the Consular Consolidated Database.

DHS-80.classNote: Traveler Entity Resolution. Uses Sex/Gender and Age. Outputs integrated into ATS to recommend further inspection. Appeal: ‘Law precludes.’

DHS-2759.classNote: Open-source intelligence for investigations. One of only two systems using Race/Ethnicity as an explicit model input (along with DHS-365). Also uses Sex/Gender and Age. Outputs include ‘flagged risk alerts, extracted identifiers, image and sentiment classification, and suggested investigative leads.’

DHS-81.classNote: Passport Anomaly Model. Uses Age as a demographic variable. Detects anomalies in passport applications.

systemwideFindings.finding: DHS failed to fill in all inventory fields, elected self-certification over third-party review, and does not notify people or their attorneys when AI has been used in their case.
Only 19% of projects reported complete documentation about training data and trustworthiness. 95% of tools contracted out to vendors. Only 16% of contracted cases reported performance metrics.
27 of 105 immigration-related use cases labeled ‘rights-impacting.’ 16 involve facial recognition or face capture.
Audited DHS AI use for cybersecurity. Found key responsible AI practices not fully implemented.
Called on DHS to suspend AI technologies for immigration enforcement that do not comply with federal requirements for responsible AI, citing violations for life-impacting decisions.
